questions.question: What happens when you assign a String from one variable to another?
options: Ownership is moved and the original is invalidated
Both variables share the same heap data
questions.explanation: For heap-allocated types like String, assignment moves ownership. The original variable becomes invalid to prevent double-free.

questions.question: You can have multiple mutable references to the same data in the same scope.
questions.explanation: Rust allows either one mutable reference OR any number of immutable references at a time — never both. This prevents data races at compile time.

questions.question: Fix this code by borrowing instead of moving:
questions.explanation: Using `&String` (or idiomatically `&str`) borrows the value without taking ownership, so s1 remains valid after the call.

questions.question: Which types implement the Copy trait and are NOT moved on assignment?
options: Scalar types like i32, bool, f64, and char
questions.explanation: Simple stack-only types implement Copy: integers, floats, booleans, characters, and tuples containing only Copy types.

questions.question: Keep both variables valid by creating a deep copy:
questions.explanation: clone() performs a deep copy of the heap data, so both s1 and s2 own independent copies.

questions.question: A slice (&[T] or &str) takes ownership of the data it references.
questions.explanation: Slices are references — they borrow a contiguous section of a collection without taking ownership.

questions.question: What does the borrow checker primarily prevent?
questions.explanation: The borrow checker enforces ownership and reference rules at compile time, preventing data races and use-after-free bugs.

questions.question: Create a string slice of the first 5 characters:
questions.code:
let s = String::from("hello world");
let hello = &s[___];
questions.explanation: String slices use byte ranges. `&s[0..5]` (or `&s[..5]`) creates a &str referencing the first 5 bytes.
